title: CSS
description: Master styling and layout — from selectors and the box model to flexbox, grid, animations, and responsive design.

lessons.title: CSS Introduction
lessons.subtitle: What CSS is, how it links to HTML, and the cascade
lessons.theory:
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the language that describes how HTML elements should look on screen, in print, or in other media. While HTML defines structure and meaning, CSS controls colors, fonts, spacing, layout, and visual effects.

You apply CSS by linking an external stylesheet, embedding styles in a <style> block, or using the style attribute (sparingly). External stylesheets are preferred: one file can style many pages, and browsers cache it for faster loads.

A CSS rule has a selector (which elements to target) and a declaration block with property–value pairs, such as color: navy or margin: 1rem. The browser matches selectors against the DOM and applies matching rules.

The cascade resolves conflicts when multiple rules target the same element. Specificity, source order, and importance (!important) determine which declaration wins. Understanding the cascade helps you predict why a style did or did not apply.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: CSS is a stylesheet language that selects HTML elements and assigns presentation properties such as color, size, and layout.

keyPoints: CSS styles HTML; it does not replace HTML structure
Rules use selectors plus property: value declarations
External .css files are the most maintainable approach
The cascade picks the winning rule when styles conflict
Rem and em scale better than fixed pixels for typography

codeExample.label: First stylesheet

quiz.question: What is the primary role of CSS on a web page?
options: To store user data in a database
To execute logic in the browser
quiz.explanation: CSS handles presentation. HTML provides structure; JavaScript adds behavior.

quiz.question: Which part of a CSS rule chooses which elements are styled?
quiz.explanation: The selector (e.g. h1 or .card) determines which elements receive the declarations.

quiz.question: Why are external stylesheets often preferred over inline styles?
options: They only work in dark mode
They separate concerns and reuse across pages
quiz.explanation: External CSS keeps markup clean and lets one file style many pages.

lessons.title: Selectors
lessons.subtitle: Type, class, ID, attribute, and combinator selectors
lessons.theory:
Selectors tell the browser which elements receive a rule. The universal selector (*) matches everything but is rarely needed for whole-page resets. Type selectors (p, h1) match elements by tag name.

Class selectors (.btn) match elements with that class attribute. Classes are reusable—use them for design patterns you apply many times. ID selectors (#header) match a single id; specificity is high, so avoid styling with IDs when a class works.

Attribute selectors target elements by attributes: input[type="email"] or a[href^="https"]. Combinators express relationships: descendant (space), child (>), adjacent sibling (+), and general sibling (~).

Pseudo-classes (:hover, :focus, :nth-child()) style elements in a particular state. Pseudo-elements (::before, ::after) let you insert decorative content. Group selectors (h1, h2, h3) share the same declarations efficiently.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A CSS selector is a pattern that matches elements in the document so declarations can be applied to them.

keyPoints: Classes (.name) are reusable; IDs (#name) should be unique per page
Descendant selectors match nested elements at any depth
Child selectors (>) only match direct children
:hover and :focus style interactive states
Specificity increases with IDs, classes, and inline styles

codeExample.label: Common selectors

quiz.question: Which selector targets elements with class="card"?
quiz.explanation: A leading dot denotes a class selector: .card

quiz.question: What does ul > li select?
options: All li elements on the page
Only li elements that are direct children of ul
li inside any ancestor of ul
quiz.explanation: The child combinator (>) matches direct children only.

quiz.question: When should you prefer a class over an ID for styling?
options: When you need the highest specificity always
When the style should apply to many elements
When the element must appear once in HTML only
quiz.explanation: Classes are reusable and keep specificity lower, making CSS easier to maintain.

lessons.title: Box Model
lessons.subtitle: Content, padding, border, margin, and box-sizing
lessons.theory:
Every element in CSS is represented as a rectangular box. The box model has four layers from inside out: content, padding, border, and margin. Width and height properties usually refer to the content area unless box-sizing says otherwise.

Padding adds space inside the border, between content and the edge. Margin adds space outside the border, separating boxes from neighbors. Borders sit between padding and margin and can have width, style, and color.

By default, width: 300px sets only the content width; padding and border add to the total footprint. That makes layout math painful. Setting box-sizing: border-box includes padding and border in the declared width and height.

Vertical margins between block elements sometimes collapse: the larger margin wins instead of both adding. Horizontal margins do not collapse. Understanding the box model is essential before flexbox and grid.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: The CSS box model describes how an element's content area, padding, border, and margin combine to occupy space on the page.

keyPoints: Content → padding → border → margin, inside to outside
border-box makes width include padding and border
margin separates elements; padding separates content from border
Block elements stack vertically by default
Margin collapse affects adjacent vertical margins

codeExample.label: Box model sizing

quiz.question: What does box-sizing: border-box change?
options: Width and height include padding and border
It converts block elements to inline
quiz.explanation: border-box makes the declared width count padding and border inside that size.

quiz.question: Where does padding appear relative to the border?
options: Inside the border, around the content
quiz.explanation: Padding is inside the border, pushing content away from the border edge.

quiz.question: Which property adds space outside the element's border?
quiz.explanation: Margin creates space outside the border between this box and others.

lessons.title: Colors
lessons.subtitle: Color formats, opacity, gradients, and contrast
lessons.theory:
CSS offers many ways to specify color. Named colors (navy, tomato) are easy to read but limited. Hex (#04AA6D) is compact and common in design systems. Functional notations rgb(), hsl(), and modern rgb() with slash syntax for alpha give precise control.

The alpha channel controls transparency. rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5) or hsl(160 84% 39% / 0.5) lets backgrounds show through. opacity on an element affects the entire element and its children, not just one property.

Linear and radial gradients are built from color stops: linear-gradient(135deg, #04AA6D, #059862). They are images under the hood, so they work with background-image.

Accessible UI needs sufficient contrast between text and background. WCAG guidelines recommend at least 4.5:1 for normal text. Tools and DevTools help you check contrast when picking colors.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: CSS color values assign foreground and background hues using names, hex, rgb, hsl, or gradients, optionally with transparency.

keyPoints: Hex, rgb(), and hsl() are the most common formats
Alpha transparency can apply to a color or whole element
Gradients use background-image, not color alone
currentColor inherits the computed text color
Contrast ratios matter for readable, accessible text

codeExample.label: Colors and gradients

quiz.question: Which notation includes hue, saturation, and lightness?
quiz.explanation: hsl() separates hue, saturation, and lightness, which is intuitive for design tweaks.

quiz.question: How do you apply a left-to-right green gradient as a background?
quiz.explanation: Gradients are background images: background-image: linear-gradient(...).

quiz.question: What is the difference between opacity and alpha in a color?
options: They are identical in every case
opacity affects the whole element; alpha can target one color value
quiz.explanation: Element opacity fades everything inside; alpha in rgba/hsl fades that specific color use.

lessons.title: Flexbox
lessons.subtitle: One-dimensional flexible layouts with flex containers
lessons.theory:
Flexbox lays out items in one dimension—either a row or a column. Apply display: flex on the parent (flex container). Direct children become flex items. The main axis follows flex-direction: row (horizontal by default) or column (vertical).

justify-content aligns items along the main axis: flex-start, center, space-between, and so on. align-items aligns on the cross axis. gap adds space between items without margin hacks.

flex-grow lets items expand to fill free space; flex-shrink lets them shrink. flex-basis sets the initial size before growing or shrinking. The shorthand flex: 1 often means flex: 1 1 0%.

flex-wrap: wrap moves items to the next line when they overflow. Use flexbox for nav bars, card rows, centering content, and distributing space in a single row or column.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Flexbox is a CSS layout module for arranging items along a main axis with flexible sizing and alignment inside a container.

keyPoints: display: flex turns an element into a flex container
justify-content aligns on the main axis
align-items aligns on the cross axis
gap replaces many margin-between-item patterns
flex: 1 is a common shorthand for flexible growth

codeExample.label: Centered navbar

quiz.question: Which property creates a flex container?
quiz.explanation: display: flex on the parent enables flexbox for its direct children.

quiz.question: justify-content: center aligns items along which axis?
quiz.explanation: justify-content controls alignment along the main axis (row or column direction).

quiz.question: What does gap do in a flex container?
quiz.explanation: gap sets consistent spacing between rows and columns of flex items.

lessons.title: Grid
lessons.subtitle: Two-dimensional layouts with rows, columns, and areas
lessons.theory:
CSS Grid layouts in two dimensions at once: rows and columns. Set display: grid on the container, then define tracks with grid-template-columns and grid-template-rows. Units like fr (fraction) distribute leftover space flexibly: 1fr 1fr makes two equal columns.

grid-template-areas names regions with ASCII art strings, which is readable for page shells (header, sidebar, main, footer). Items are placed with grid-area or by spanning lines using grid-column and grid-row.

gap (or row-gap and column-gap) spaces tracks consistently. minmax(200px, 1fr) creates responsive columns that never shrink below 200px but grow to share space.

Grid excels at full-page layouts, dashboards, and photo galleries. Combine grid for the page skeleton with flexbox inside components for one-dimensional toolbars or card footers.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: CSS Grid is a two-dimensional layout system that places items into explicit rows and columns on a track-based grid.

keyPoints: display: grid enables row and column tracks
fr units divide free space proportionally
grid-template-areas maps named layout regions
minmax() builds responsive track sizes
Grid pairs well with flexbox inside cells

codeExample.label: Page layout grid

quiz.question: What does the fr unit represent in grid-template-columns?
quiz.explanation: fr allocates a share of leftover space in the grid container.

quiz.question: Which property defines named layout regions like "header" and "main"?
quiz.explanation: grid-template-areas assigns names to cells; items use grid-area to sit in them.

quiz.question: When is Grid usually a better choice than Flexbox alone?
options: For a single row of buttons only
quiz.explanation: Grid shines when you need both rows and columns aligned at once.

lessons.title: Animations
lessons.subtitle: Transitions, @keyframes, transforms, and motion preferences
lessons.theory:
CSS can animate property changes smoothly with transition. Specify transition-property, transition-duration, transition-timing-function, or the shorthand transition: transform 0.3s ease. Transitions run when a property changes—often on :hover or a class toggle.

@keyframes defines multi-step animations. An animation rule references a name, duration, and iteration count: animation: fade-in 0.6s ease forwards. Use percentages or from/to to set styles along the timeline.

transform moves, scales, and rotates elements without triggering layout-heavy changes: translateX(10px), scale(1.05), rotate(5deg). Prefer transform and opacity for performant motion.

Respect users who prefer reduced motion: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) can disable or shorten animations. Meaningful motion should enhance feedback, not distract from content.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: CSS animations and transitions change property values over time, often using @keyframes and transform for visual effects.

keyPoints: transition animates between two states on property change
@keyframes defines named multi-step animations
transform and opacity are GPU-friendly for motion
animation shorthand sets name, duration, and timing
prefers-reduced-motion improves accessibility

codeExample.label: Hover transition and keyframes

quiz.question: What triggers a CSS transition?
options: A change in a property value from one state to another
quiz.explanation: Transitions interpolate when a property changes, such as on hover or class toggle.

quiz.question: Where are animation steps defined?
quiz.explanation: @keyframes holds the percentage or from/to keyframes for a named animation.

quiz.question: Which properties are generally best for smooth performance?
quiz.explanation: transform and opacity often composite on the GPU without expensive layout recalculations.

lessons.title: Responsive Design
lessons.subtitle: Media queries, fluid units, and mobile-first layouts
lessons.theory:
Responsive design adapts layouts to different screen sizes and contexts. The viewport meta tag in HTML lets mobile browsers scale the layout width correctly. Without it, many phones render pages as if they were desktop width.

Media queries apply CSS when conditions match: @media (min-width: 768px) { ... }. Mobile-first means you write base styles for small screens, then add min-width queries for larger breakpoints.

Fluid units help: % widths relative to parents, vw/vh relative to viewport, rem relative to root font size. max-width on images (max-width: 100%; height: auto) prevents overflow in narrow columns.

Flexible grids use repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr)) or flex-wrap so cards reflow without dozens of breakpoints. Test on real devices and narrow DevTools viewports, not only desktop widths.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Responsive design uses flexible layouts, relative units, and media queries so interfaces work well across screen sizes.

keyPoints: Mobile-first uses min-width media queries
viewport meta enables correct scaling on phones
rem and % scale better than fixed px alone
max-width: 100% keeps images inside containers
auto-fit grids reflow cards without manual breakpoints

codeExample.label: Responsive card grid

quiz.question: What does a mobile-first min-width query do?
options: Applies styles only below the breakpoint
Applies styles when the viewport is at least that wide
quiz.explanation: min-width: 768px means the rules apply at 768px and wider.

quiz.question: Why use max-width: 100% on images in responsive layouts?
options: To prevent images from overflowing narrow containers
quiz.explanation: Images shrink with their container instead of forcing horizontal scroll.

quiz.question: Which HTML meta tag helps mobile browsers use the device width?
quiz.explanation: viewport width=device-width maps the layout width to the device screen.
